title: Factorization rule for multitime correlations in non-Markovian open quantum systems
abstract: Experiments performed on quantum systems often measure multitime correlation functions. When quantum systems are weakly coupled to their environment, the time evolution of such correlation functions can be reduced to that of the reduced density matrix by the quantum regression theorem (QRT). While no QRT is available for general non-Markovian open quantum systems, we show that for time-independent Hamiltonians and finite memory times $τ_c$, an exact factorization rule exists that relates higher-order multitime correlations to products of lower-order correlations. Consequently, all information needed to reconstruct $n$-time correlations is contained in a temporal volume of $\mathcal{O}(τ_c^n)$. On the example of quantum dots coupled to phonons, we demonstrate that this factorization makes numerical calculations of multitime correlations extremely efficient and even enables semianalytical solutions in systems where the standard QRT breaks down.
summary: This paper presents a new exact factorization rule for calculating multitime correlation functions in non-Markovian systems. By breaking down complex n-time correlations into simpler lower-order parts, the method achieves massive computational speed-ups without sacrificing accuracy. This is particularly useful for studying the optical properties of quantum dots coupled to phonon environments.
main_problem: Calculating multitime correlation functions in non-Markovian open quantum systems is computationally expensive because the breakdown of the Quantum Regression Theorem (QRT) requires long-time, brute-force simulations.
main_result: The authors derive an exact factorization rule that decomposes n-th order correlations into products of lower-order correlations, providing a speed-up of two to three orders of magnitude while maintaining exact accuracy.
method: The method uses an eigenvector decomposition of the stationary dynamical map within a Markovian embedding framework to decouple time steps.
model_or_system: The study focuses on non-Markovian open quantum systems with time-independent Hamiltonians and finite memory times, specifically using semiconductor quantum dots coupled to a super-Ohmic phonon bath as a primary example.
key_observables: Multitime correlation functions, emission spectra S(omega), second-order coherence G(2)(t, tau), and integrated second-order coherence g(2)[0].
important_parameters: Memory time (tau_c), phonon dynamics (picosecond scale), radiative decay (nanosecond scale), and temperature (4 K).
main_assumptions: The Hamiltonian is time-independent, the initial system-environment state is an uncorrelated product, and the environment has a finite memory time.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the stationary dynamical map and the visual concept of factorization; Figure 2 compares the accuracy of the factorization method against brute-force and QRT for emission spectra; Figure 3 shows time-integrated second-order coherence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of non-Markovian correlations, derives the mathematical factorization rule using Liouvillian eigenbasis decomposition, applies the method to quantum dots coupled to phonons, and validates the efficiency and accuracy against brute-force simulations.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful computational shortcut for simulating complex non-Markovian dynamics, allowing for the efficient calculation of high-order correlations and spectra that were previously too expensive to compute.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The dynamical map for density matrix evolution becomes stationary after the memory time t ≥τc, which en- ables exact long-time extrapolation of non-Markovian dynam- ics. (b) Exact factorization of a two-time correlation function when t2 −t1 ≥τc, i.e., t1 and t2 differ by at least the memory time τc. Propagation over 2τ 2 c contains all information needed to reconstruct two-time correlations with arbitrary time ar- guments.

FIG. 2. (a) Emission spectrum of a weakly cw-driven QD with non-Markovian coupling to phonons. Standard QRT predicts the phonon sideband on the wrong side of the zero-phonon line. Using factorization the complete information is con- tained in a temporal volume of τ 2 c . (b) Exemplary emission spectrum for resonant pulsed excitation.

FIG. 3. (a) Time-integrated second-order coherence for a res- onant pulse with σ = 5 ps and pulse area of π, resulting in g(2)[0] = 0.032. For other parameters, see appendix. (b) Ra- tio of photon counts stemming from a single pulse and subse- quent pulses at different pulse areas.
